title: Dismantling Syria’s chemical-weapons stocks and legacy
gist: Syria's transitional government and the OPCW face a multi-year, hazardous, underfunded effort to track and destroy the Assad regime's chemical-weapons legacy, complicated by landmines, civil unrest, active jihadist groups, and an undeclared programme far larger than originally listed.

insights.claim: Syria's 2013 OPCW declaration listed 12 production facilities and 1,300 tonnes of chemical weapons or precursors, of which 1,330 tonnes were verified destroyed in 2014, but the inventory was widely suspected to be incomplete.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: The official baseline understated the real arsenal; any 'completion' framing of Syrian disarmament should be treated skeptically.
insights.quote: Its initial declaration to the OPCW listed 12 production facilities, 1,300 tonnes of CW or precursors and a number of CW storage facilities.
insights.locator: Tracing Syria's CW past section, 'Following the chemical attack in Ghouta'

insights.claim: Since the Assad regime fell in December 2024, evidence has surfaced of possibly 100 additional CW-related facilities, including mustard gas, DF and sarin precursors, with the full programme size still unclear.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Regime collapse exposed a far larger hidden program; disarmament scope and timeline are open-ended, not converging.
insights.quote: There may be 100 additional facilities related to the former CW programme
insights.locator: Tracing Syria's CW past section, 'Since the fall of the Assad regime'

insights.claim: Syria's declared CW programme is small versus the former Soviet Union's 40,000 tonnes but larger than Libya's 23 tonnes of mustard agent declared in 2004.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Benchmarking against Soviet and Libyan cases frames the disposal burden as substantial but not unprecedented.
insights.locator: Tracing Syria's CW past section, 'Compared to the declared programme of the former Soviet Union'

insights.claim: The OPCW Technical Secretariat reports 19 unresolved issues in Syria's declaration, including the disappearance of 75 undeclared cylinders from a previously reported location.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Missing cylinders signal possible diversion; a proliferation and chemical-terrorism risk that outlives the formal disarmament process.
insights.quote: 19 issues remain unresolved, including the disappearance of 75 undeclared cylinders from a location previously reported to the OPCW.
insights.locator: A challenging task and environment section, 'According to the OPCW Technical Secretariat'

insights.claim: Landmines and unexploded ordnance around CW sites require armoured mechanical assets for clearance before OPCW inspectors can safely enter, slowing the disarmament process.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Physical site hazards, not just politics, gate the timeline; demand for demining and EOD capability is a concrete bottleneck.
insights.locator: A challenging task and environment section, 'the presence of unexploded ordnance'

insights.claim: The Islamic State remains active in Syria and has a track record of using chemical weapons, raising the risk of CW acquisition by non-state armed groups amid weak site security.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Active jihadist groups plus unguarded sites make non-state CW acquisition a live risk; a regional security tail-risk to monitor.
insights.quote: the Islamic State (IS) remains active in Syria and has a track record of using CWs
insights.locator: A challenging task and environment section, 'ongoing domestic unrest in Syria'

insights.claim: Civil unrest forced OPCW personnel to leave Syria in March 2026 with field visits suspended until May 2026, demonstrating that on-and-off access will repeatedly stall verification.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Verification progress is hostage to Syrian internal stability; expect a stop-start, multi-year cadence rather than steady completion.
insights.locator: The long tail section, 'because of continuous civil unrest'

insights.claim: Syria's chemical-disarmament operations require at least €12.5 million in 2027, a burden made harder by the second Trump administration's USAID budget cuts that defunded groups working on chemical disarmament.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: US aid retrenchment directly weakens disarmament capacity; funding shortfalls, not just security, may stall the program.
insights.quote: the chemical-disarmament operations will require an investment of at least €12.5 million in 2027
insights.locator: A challenging task and environment section, 'Moreover, the chemical-disarmament operations'

insights.claim: Syria's elimination plan relies on an international task force of Canada, France, Germany, Qatar, Türkiye, the UK, the US and the OPCW to provide training and equipment for containment, transfer and destruction of CW.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Multinational backing exists but spreads dependence across many states' continued commitment; a coalition-cohesion risk.
insights.locator: The long tail section, 'Its plan to eliminate the CW includes an international task force'

insights.claim: Since November 2025 the OPCW has reopened its Syria office, visited over 20 sites including previously unexamined Barzeh and Jamraya facilities, and received 37 sealed boxes containing over 60,000 pages of documents.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Concrete investigative progress is underway; the document trove is the basis for both verification and future prosecutions.
insights.quote: The Syrian authorities also handed over 37 sealed cardboard boxes containing over 60,000 pages of documents.

insights.claim: A redeployed OPCW team found dozens of undeclared chemical munitions, including rockets of the same type used in the 2013 Ghouta attacks, at undeclared coastal and central sites.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Fresh discoveries linking munitions to Ghouta strengthen both the proliferation concern and the evidentiary case for accountability.
insights.quote: dozens of undeclared chemicals munitions such as aerial bombs and rockets, as well as separately found chemicals and related equipment
insights.locator: The long tail section, 'A team has since been redeployed'

insights.claim: Securing evidence quickly is critical for judicial accountability, with at least 18 people arrested over the Ghouta attacks and the OPCW feeding evidence to the International, Impartial and Independent Mechanism for criminal proceedings.
insights.speaker: IISS
insights.so_what: Disarmament and war-crimes prosecution are racing the same clock; evidence preservation is the linchpin for accountability outcomes.
insights.quote: Winning the race to secure evidence will be critical for Syria’s road towards judicial accountability for the use of CWs.
insights.locator: The long tail section, 'A final challenge is related to criminal justice'
